metaTitle: Anatomy of a Kill Chain in a Single PCAP
metaDescription: A walkthrough of one sample capture where a single host runs the full kill chain — recon, lateral movement, C2 beaconing, and exfil — all triaged in your browser.
title: Anatomy of a kill chain, in a single packet capture
dek: One host in this sample capture runs the entire playbook — from first scan to data leaving the building. Here's how PacketPilot reconstructs it, and how you can watch it happen in your own browser.
author: PacketPilot

blocks.p: The capture we're triaging is about 100,000 packets — 99,079 flows across 547 hosts, spanning roughly 63 hours. It's the kind of noisy, real-world traffic where anything interesting hides in a sea of ordinary web and DNS. Dropping it into PacketPilot, the engine doesn't hand back a packet list; it hands back twelve high-severity findings, correlated into six incidents and ranked by risk. The worst scores 89 out of 100.

The whole point of triage is to answer one question — "where do I look first?" — before you ever open a single packet. Here, one host answers it loudly. And every bit of the analysis below ran locally in the browser tab: the capture was never uploaded anywhere.

Five of the six incidents are isolated hygiene problems (we'll get to them). The sixth is a story. The engine attributes seven of the findings to a single internal host — 10.13.37.7 — and correlates them into one incident scored 89. Individually each finding is suspicious; together they trace the shape of an intrusion, from first contact to data leaving the network.

table.caption: The findings attributed to host 10.13.37.7, ordered as a kill chain.
rows: Host sweep — 10.13.37.7 probed 24 hosts on port 445 (SMB)
RDP sessions to 6 internal hosts on port 3389
DNS tunneling — 40 high-entropy queries to the internal resolver

blocks.p: It starts quietly. The engine flags a host sweep: 10.13.37.7 reaching 24 different hosts on TCP 445. One host touching that many peers on the SMB port, in a short window, isn't a file share — it's someone mapping the network. That's Discovery (T1046).

Next, the same host hammers 10.66.0.1 on SSH — 30 connection attempts to port 22. The brute-force detector is gated to interactive auth services and counts attempts, so ordinary retries don't trip it; thirty against one target does. That's Credential Access (T1110).

With a foothold, 10.13.37.7 opens RDP sessions to six internal hosts on 3389. One internal machine fanning out admin sessions to many others is the classic lateral-movement signature (T1021) — and it's exactly the pattern the detector is tuned to catch without flagging a busy jump box.

Then comes the tell-tale rhythm. The host makes 7,613 connections to an external address, 45.77.13.37:443, at a near-perfect ~30-second cadence — a jitter coefficient of variation of just 0.014. Humans and normal software aren't that regular; malware calling home is. That machine-regular heartbeat is command-and-control beaconing (T1071). Running alongside it, 40 high-entropy DNS queries to the internal resolver betray DNS tunneling (T1071.004) — a second, quieter channel out.

Finally, data moves. About 1 MB leaves for the same C2 at 45.77.13.37, and another 1.2 MB goes to a second external host, 185.220.101.5 — an address on a well-known anonymizing network. Large, asymmetric, outbound transfers to external peers are Exfiltration (T1048). The story closes where these stories usually do: with the data already gone.

sections.heading: The same beacon, seen from the other side

blocks.p: One detail is worth pausing on. The engine also raises a separate finding on 45.77.13.37 itself — a SYN-flood-shaped pattern of 7,613 half-open connections to its port 443. That's not a second attack; it's the same 7,613 beacons, viewed from the server's perspective. Seeing the client-side beacon and the server-side connection storm line up is a clean confirmation that the ~30-second heartbeat is real, and not a detector artifact.
The other five incidents aren't part of the intrusion — they're the ordinary exposure a real network accumulates, surfaced in the same pass:

bullets: Cleartext credentials — HTTP Basic auth from 10.0.0.50 to an internal web server on port 80, and an FTP login from 10.0.0.51 on port 21. Passwords on the wire, in plaintext (T1552).
Plaintext PII — a credit-card number crossing 10.0.0.52 to port 80 over unencrypted HTTP (T1040).
Exposed remote access — an external host, 45.77.13.38, reached RDP on an internal machine (10.0.0.42:3389), an internal-to-external boundary crossing that shouldn't be possible (T1133).

blocks.p: None of these would stop you in a raw packet list. Ranked next to the kill chain, they read as a to-do list.

sections.heading: Triage, not packets — and nothing left the browser

blocks.p: The reason to do this in a triage tool instead of scrolling Wireshark is speed and framing: 100,000 packets became six ranked incidents and one obvious place to start, in seconds. Wireshark is still where you go to prove the details — but not to decide where to look.

And the reason to do it in PacketPilot specifically is where the work happens. The entire analysis ran in a Rust-to-WebAssembly engine inside the browser tab. This capture is full of internal IPs, credentials, and a credit-card number; none of it was uploaded anywhere. That's exactly why a browser-local tool is usable on captures you could never paste into a cloud analyzer.
Everything above is the bundled sample — you can reproduce it yourself in about ten seconds, no account required. Open the live sample and watch the same kill chain assemble.
